2. Explanation of Inquiry-Based Learning.desc:
- It is a [learning and teaching method] that prioritizes students' questions, ideas and analyses.

- From a [student point-of-view], inquiry-based learning focuses on investigating an open question or problem. They must use evidence-based reasoning and creative problem-solving to reach a conclusion, which they must defend or present.

- From a [teacher point-of-view], inquiry-based teaching focuses on moving students beyond general curiosity into the realms of critical thinking and understanding. You must encourage students to ask questions and support them through the investigation process, understanding when to begin and how to structure an inquiry activity.

- By using methods such as guided research, document analysis and question-and-answer sessions, you can run inquiry activities in the form of:
  - Case studies
  - Group projects
  - Research projects
  - Field work, especially for science sessions
  - Unique exercises tailored to your students

3. Nature and Meaning of Inclusive Schoo.desc:
- ISBI is a [pedagogy] which best enables students to experience the processes of knowledge creation.

- Its key attributes are:
  - [learning stimulated by inquiry]
  - a [student-centred approach]
  - a move to [self-directed learning]
  - an [active approach to learning]

- Students should develop [research skills] and become [life-long learners].

- Evidence shows ISBI can enhance [student engagement, academic achievement, and higher order learning outcomes]. Benefits can also accrue for teachers through the integration of teaching and research.

- Ghana has adopted Education For All (EFA) which states that to the maximum extent appropriate, children with disabilities are educated with children who are not handicapped, with the use of supplementary aids and services.

- On the basis of inclusion and exclusion, ISBI can be described as: [embracing everyone and making a commitment to provide each student with the inalienable right to belong].

- ISBI involves students of [diverse backgrounds and/or abilities] working together to explore significant concepts and issues by using a process that supports collaboration, discussion and reflection.

- To facilitate ISBI, teachers should first establish [clear guidelines for group discussions] and be sensitive to the needs of specific students.

- It is a way to [integrate research and teaching] as both students and teachers are 'compatriots in the search for knowledge' (Justice et al., 2007a).

- **School restructuring efforts for ISBI include**:
  - Heterogeneous and cooperative group arrangements
  - Individualized approaches to curriculum and assessment
  - Collaboration among staff, students, parents and community
  - Opportunity for students to think and be creative
  - Facilitating students' social skills

1. Meaning of Inclusive Education.desc:
- Inclusive education is when [all students], regardless of any challenges they may have, are placed in [age-appropriate general education classes] that are in their own neighborhood schools, to receive [high-quality instruction, interventions, and supports] that enable them to meet success in the core curriculum (Bui, Quirk, Almazan, & Valenti, 2010; Alquraini & Gut, 2012).

- The school and classroom operate on the premise that students with disabilities are as [fundamentally competent] as students without disabilities.

- This movement is related to legislation that students receive their education in the [least restrictive environment (LRE)]. This means they are with peers without disabilities to the maximum degree possible, with general education the placement of first choice for all students.

- Inclusive education denotes what happens primarily through [accepting, understanding, and attending to students' differences and diversity], which can include physical, cognitive, academic, social and emotional differences.

- The driving principle is to make all students feel [welcomed, appropriately challenged, and supported] in their efforts.

ex: Confirmation Inquiry: teacher provides question, answer, and method

1. Types of Inclusive School-Based Inqui.desc:
- There are different kinds of inclusive school-based inquiry, which become decreasingly structured and suit different classrooms.

- **[Confirmation Inquiry]**: you give students a [question, its answer and the method] of reaching this answer. Their goal is to build investigation and critical-thinking skills.

- **[Structured Inquiry]**: you give students an [open question and an investigation method]. They must use the method to craft an evidence-backed conclusion.

- **[Guided Inquiry]**: you give students an [open question]. Typically, in groups, they design investigation methods to reach a conclusion.

- **[Open Inquiry]**: you give students [time and support]. They pose original questions that they investigate through their own methods, and eventually present their results.

3. Motivating Learners.desc:
- One of the primary reasons for advocating an inclusive inquiry approach is because it is thought to [motivate learners more strongly].

- Motivation affects the [amount of time and energy] that people are willing to devote to learning.

- Tasks must be challenging but at the proper level of difficulty to remain motivating – if they are [too easy students will be bored], while if they are [too hard, students will become frustrated].

- Learners will be better stimulated and motivated to learn by [sparking their curiosity]. Confronting students with a state of perplexity prompts them to seek questions and evidence.

- Learners are also motivated when they can see the [usefulness and relevance] of what they are learning – especially in their local community.

2. Theoretical Support for ISBI (Constru.desc:
- The theoretical support for ISBI stems from widely accepted research on student learning, with its roots in [constructivism].

- **[Constructivism]**: the key tenet is that an individual learner must [actively construct knowledge and skills] (Bruner, 1990).

- There are two main forms of constructivism: [cognitive] and [social].

- **[Cognitivist Constructivism]**: draws mainly on Piaget's theory. Individuals must construct their own knowledge and build knowledge through [experience], creating schemas or mental modes.

- **[Social Constructivism]**: places more emphasis on the [social context] of learning (Vygotsky). Cultural history, social context and language play an important role in development. Vygotsky's concept of the [zone of proximal development] argues that individuals can, with the help of a more experienced peer, master concepts they cannot understand on their own.

- According to constructivist theory, effective teaching must offer experiences that:
  - build on what students already know
  - encourage students to become active, self-directed learners
  - provide authentic learning opportunities
  - involve students working together in small groups

3. Benefits of Inclusive School-Based In.desc:
- **[Promotes a Deeper Understanding of Content]**:
  - By delving into a concept through inquiry, students see it as more than a simple rule, idea or formula
  - Students understand [how the idea was developed, why the rule works, and when to properly apply it]
  - The process of asking open questions and solving them through original strategies empowers students to [take ownership of their learning]

- **[Helps Make Learning Rewarding]**:
  - Inquiry helps students see the [intrinsic rewards] of learning, rather than learning only for parental or teacher approval
  - It shows students how [fulfilling the act of discovery] is, and that theorizing a new strategy is a reward itself
  - Students grow to [enjoy the learning process] itself, not just external approval

2. Benefits of Inclusive School-Based In.desc:
- As we make efforts to build skills to help students reach a high level of thinking, inclusive school-based inquiry delivers several benefits to students and teachers.

- **[Reinforces Curriculum Content]**:
  - You can use inquiry-based learning to [reinforce relevant content] and improve understanding of core concepts
  - This is due to curiosity's effect on the brain – when a concept sparks curiosity, there is increased activity in the [hippocampus], the region responsible for memory creation
  - When students show more curiosity, use their questions to introduce inquiry activities, helping them retain essential information

- **["Warms Up" the Brain for Learning]**:
  - Running a brief inquiry activity to start class helps students [absorb information throughout the day]
  - Curiosity [prepares the brain for learning], allowing students to become more proficient at understanding and remembering skills and concepts
  - An easy way to inspire curiosity is by launching a [surprise inquiry activity] related to a recent topic students found interesting

1. Characteristics of Inclusive School-B.desc:
- Inclusive school-based inquiry has unique characteristics that distinguish it from traditional teaching approaches.

- **[School Climate]**:
  - [Responsibility for all students] is shared among all staff
  - The school environment supports teachers in working [collaboratively] (e.g., common planning time, co-teaching)

- **[Curriculum, instruction, and assessment]**:
  - Students with special learning needs may receive instruction in the [general education classroom] and participate in extracurricular activities with necessary supports
  - All students participate in state and district assessments with needed or approved [adaptations and modifications]
  - All staff participate in developing and implementing a [school-wide behavior plan]
  - A [team approach] ensures that each student receives the appropriate help when needed

- **[Staff development]**:
  - Professional development activities are aimed at [building capacity] by enhancing the skills of all staff to promote students' access to the general education curriculum

4. Benefits of Inclusive School-Based In.desc:
- **[Builds Initiative and Self-Direction]**:
  - Students improve transferable skills related to [initiative and self-direction]
  - Students learn how to [ask questions, investigate, discuss, collaborate, cooperate and reach their own conclusions]
  - Self-guided inquiry and analysis synthesizes development of these skills for use in postsecondary school and beyond

- **[Works in almost any classroom]**:
  - You can repurpose activities for almost any classroom regardless of grade and individual skill levels
  - You can [adapt the pace and content] to suit student needs
  - You can appeal to students who struggle with traditional lessons
  - You can deliver [exercises that greatly differ] using distinct content and investigation methods

- **[Offers differentiated instruction]**:
  - Inquiry-based learning allows you to use [differentiated instruction strategies], appealing to diverse learning styles
  - Students can work by themselves, or as part of small or large groups
  - Inquiry involves methods such as [discussion and guided research]
  - You can provide content in form of [text, audio, video and virtual or physical manipulatives]

4. Ways to Identify Learners with Divers.desc:
- Language development involves [comprehension, expression and articulation]. Verbal comprehension develops before verbal expression.

- **[Verbal Comprehension]**:
  - These children may:
    - Have difficulty understanding [long and complex sentences] or oral instructions
    - [Misunderstand the meaning] of questions and give irrelevant answers
    - Be unable to understand a [story or a cartoon video]

- **[Verbal Expression]**:
  - These children may:
    - Lack in [vocabulary] and usually give short and simple responses
    - Speak with [incorrect sentence structure or grammar]
    - Have difficulty [expressing themselves properly] and be disorganised in verbal responses
    - Find it difficult to [recall a simple event] or convey a simple message

- **[Articulation/Stuttering Problems]**:
  - They may [mispronounce words], e.g., saying "sand" as "and"
  - They may have [stuttering problems], e.g., "Can ...Can... Can... I go to toilet?"

3. Ways to Identify Learners with Divers.desc:
- **[Global Learning Abilities]**:
  - Children with relatively weak global learning abilities have [significantly weaker performance] in every aspect compared with other children of the same age
  - They are usually [slower in acquiring new skills and knowledge], more difficult to adapt to new environments, more reluctant to take challenges and tend to be more dependent
  - They have difficulty [understanding and grasping the content] of subjects or fail to generalise acquired knowledge
  - They take a [longer time to learn and practise new skills]
  - They are constantly in need of [individual guidance] from teachers

- **[Word Learning]**:
  - Some children may have problems in certain subject areas only, with a common problem being in word learning
  - It is common for pre-primary children to have [mirror writing, reversal of word parts and addition or omission of strokes] when learning to write
  - These children may:
    - Copy words with [difficulty], are slow, and often make mistakes in copying
    - Have [mirror writing] (e.g., "b" becomes "d" and "p" becomes "q")
    - [Forget easily] how to read or write words even with repeated practice
    - Often [mix up words] with similar sounds, meanings or written forms
    - Be [slow in reading] and sometimes skip words or lines

8. Ways to Identify Learners with Divers.desc:
- **[Emotions]**:
  - **[Throwing tantrums]**: Young children have immature self-control leading to frequent tantrums. Referral may be necessary if they show destructive and aggressive behaviour including hurting themselves or others
  - **[Anxiousness]**: Some children get anxious easily. They appear uneasy and restless when faced with strangers, unfamiliar environment, or are the focus of attention
    - These children may: fear going to school, appear nervous when talking, fidget when asked to answer questions, speak in soft voice, avoid eye contact
  - **[Depression mood]**: If children stay gloomy for a prolonged period affecting learning or daily life, closer examination is needed
    - These children may: appear persistently sad or sullen, show no interest in activities, cry easily, have poor appetite

- **[Social Skills]**:
  - Some children have difficulties in [communicating and interacting with people] as well as adapting to social rules
  - These children may:
    - [Rarely make friends] and prefer to play on their own
    - Be inadequate in social communication skills, often exhibit [inappropriate behaviour or speech]
    - [Rarely share] food, toys or their interests with others
    - Tend to be [self-centered] and insensitive to others' needs and feelings
    - Talk only about topics they like, ignoring others' responses, resulting in [one-way communication]
    - Lack eye contact, speak in awkward and flat tone, [rarely use facial expressions or gestures]

2. Response to Intervention (RTI).desc:
- Response to Intervention (RTI) is a relatively new approach to [identifying students with academic needs] that has gained increasing interest.

- RTI emphasizes ["student outcomes instead of student deficits"] and provides early and more immediate support for students' academic needs by screening students as early as kindergarten.

- This strategy is used to identify and provide [early intervention activities] so that students who are struggling academically can participate and progress in general education curriculum.

- Students who are not responding to traditional instruction are provided additional academic support through [individualized and small-group instruction] provided by teachers who use research-based strategies.

10. Strategies for Handling Learners wit.desc:
- Professional teachers should adopt various strategies to teach learners with diverse needs and abilities in their class.

- **[Information and Assistance]**:
  - [Gather information] about the nature of the exceptional student's difference and how that difference might affect the learning process
  - [Seek assistance] from district special education or resource experts

- **[Equipment and Materials]**:
  - Use [specialized equipment] (typewriters, computers, print enlarger, Braille material, etc.) to allow students to function at an optimum level
  - [Individualize the curriculum] by adapting materials and teaching strategies to better meet the needs of exceptional students
  - [Remove physical and psychological barriers] that limit exceptional students' ability to succeed

- **[Communication with LEP Students]**:
  - Attempt to communicate with LEP students through [gestures, pictures, and any words they know] from the students' native language
  - Take time to teach students some [key words, phrases, or gestures] so they can build peer relationships
  - [Encourage other students] to include LEP students in their activities
  - Always [maintain a positive attitude], even though attempting to communicate may be challenging

- **[Classroom Arrangement]**:
  - [Arrange learners according to their disabilities]. Those who are short sighted sit in the front
  - An inclusive classroom should be well equipped with [assistive devices]

- **[Peer and Volunteer Support]**:
  - Allow [teacher aide/volunteer] to take notes for student
  - Allow classroom peers to make [carbon copies of classwork] for the student

- **[Test Modification]**:
  - Reduce the number of exams to [open-book exams]
  - [Allow more time] for regular exam
  - Reduce the length of the regular exam
  - Use more [objective items] (fewer essay items)
  - Give some exam [orally]
  - [Write down test items] for students
  - [Read test items] to student
  - [Substitute assignment] for test
  - [Enlarge test item] print
  - [Allow use of computer]

- **[Assignment Modification]**:
  - Provide more [detailed directions]
  - [Repeat instructions]
  - Give instructions through [several channels] (oral, written, etc.)
  - Provide materials that are [programmed/self-checking]
  - [Brief student on major points] before starting an assignment
  - [Allow more time] for regular assignments
  - [Reduce length] of regular assignments
  - [Break assignment] into a series of smaller assignments
  - [Reduce reading level] of the regular assignment (edit, reword)
  - [Change format] of the instructional materials
  - Use [different format materials] to teach the same content
  - Provide [study aids] (hints, cue cards, guides, calculators, computers)
  - Provide [hands-on activities/physical assignments]
  - Allow [oral presentations/reports/projects/games], etc.

1. Who is a Diverse Learner?.desc:
- Diversity is a part of the nature of the human species, and students are and always have been [different from one another] in a variety of ways (Banks et al., 2005).

- Many diverse learners have historically been [marginalized] by the education system and may have been recipients of practices that served to alienate, segregate, and reject them.

- A diverse learner can be described as:
  - A learner that is [racially, ethnically, culturally, economically and or linguistically diverse]
  - A learner who [learns differently from the majority]

- The purpose of recognizing diverse learners is to acknowledge the need to [teach different students differently].

- Teachers must be prepared to identify diverse students' [strengths, weaknesses, aspirations, limitations, and special needs].

- Today's classrooms must [celebrate diversity]. Most classrooms include students who have documented intellectual, physical, and/or emotional exceptionalities.

- Under the Education for All policy (EFA), children with disabilities must be educated in the [least restrictive environment (LRE)], or an educational setting that is as similar as possible to the one in which children who do not have a disability are educated.

- Today, [mainstreaming] has been replaced by [inclusion], as children with special needs are taught full-time in a general education classroom by a regular education teacher and specialists.

    - Require assistance in [buttoning up clothes or tying shoelaces]

    - Be [restless in seat], fidget with their legs, stretch their bodies, or frequently drop things
    - Be [excessively talkative] and not able to work or play quietly
    - Be [impatient]; dislike taking turns and being unable to wait quietly in queue
    - Have relatively weak [self-control], tend to be impulsive; often interrupt a conversation or answer questions without raising hands

- **[Oppositional Behaviour]**:

  - These children may:

    - [Refuse to follow instructions] or compromise and often argue with others

6. Ways to Identify Learners with Divers.desc:
- Attention refers to a child's ability to [concentrate on a specific object or activity], such as reading, talking with people and playing games in class.

- Children's attention control improves with age:
  - [Under 3 years old]: cannot control their attention well
  - [3 to 4 years old]: can concentrate with external prompts or adult's guidance
  - [5 to 6 years old]: begin to control their attention and able to concentrate

- An average child of [2 to 5 years] can sustain attention for about 2 to 5 minutes
- An average child of [6 to 10 years] can sustain attention for about 8 to 10 minutes

- Children with a short attention span have difficulty concentrating especially at activities that require sustained concentration.

- These children may:
  - Have difficulty [concentrating during classes] and are easily distracted (e.g., looking out of windows, distracted by sounds)
  - Have difficulty [concentrating on class work] and make careless mistakes (e.g., missing items when doing worksheets)
  - [Not concentrate when talking with others]; often unable to follow instructions for not paying attention
  - Appear to be [forgetful or careless] and often lose their belongings

9. Ways to Identify Learners with Divers.desc:
- Family factors such as [family conflicts, financial difficulties and child neglect] also affect children's learning, emotion and behaviour.

- **[Behaviour and Emotional conditions]**:
  - Being [emotionally unstable], appearing anxious, agitated, irritable or depressed
  - Being [asocial], reluctant to talk with people or participate in activities
  - Showing [destructive or aggressive behaviour]
  - Showing [regressive and childish behaviour] inappropriate for their age
  - Exhibiting [insecure behaviour] by either over-clinging to adults or withdrawing from them
  - Being [frightened of their parents or caregivers], even reluctant to go home

- **[Physical appearance]**:
  - Appearing to be [tired, losing appetite]
  - Being [underweight]
  - Having severe rashes or skin problems, or being deprived of suitable medical treatment
  - Being [untidy and dirty], or dressing inappropriately

- **[Parents' attitude towards children]**:
  - **[Extreme Harshness]**: constantly shouting at, scolding, threatening, or using harsh discipline
  - **[Lack of Concern]**: detached and aloof towards children, treating them differently from siblings
  - **[Unreasonable Expectations]**: having overly rigid expectations, asking them to assume inappropriate responsibilities

- **[Learning impact]**:
  - Being [absent from school] frequently
  - Fail to hand in class assignments
  - Show a [marked deterioration in learning ability]

- **[Family Situations]**:
  - **[Family background]**: single parent, step-parent, new immigrants, low-income family, parents unemployed
  - **[Health conditions]**: alcoholism, substance use, gambling problems, mental health issues
  - **[Other conditions]**: frequent family conflicts, domestic violence, crisis or tension in the family, frequent changes of residence

5. Meeting the Needs of Learners with Di.desc:
- The [environment, materials and schedule] are all things that can be adjusted to promote access, participation and benefit.

- **[Environment]**:
  - Changes in [lighting, noise level, visual and auditory input, physical arrangement] of the room or equipment and accessibility of materials are important considerations
  - The environment must be designed to meet the [individual needs of the child], encourage the child to make choices, use materials independently and be actively involved

- **[Materials Modifications]**:
  - Materials can be physically adapted by increasing:
    - [Stability] (Dycem™ or Velcro™ on or under materials)
    - [Ease of handling] (adding handles, making materials larger)
    - [Accessibility] (developing a hand splint, attaching elastic cord so objects can be easily retrieved)
    - [Visual clarity] or distinctiveness (adding contrast or specialized lighting)
    - [Size]

- **[Assistive Technology]**:
  - Legal definition: "any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially off the shelf, modified, or customized that is used to [increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities] of children"
  - Assistive technology (AT) [opens the door to learning] for many children with disabilities
  - For a child with significant developmental challenges, technology may be the [only avenue to express ideas], play with a toy or demonstrate understanding
  - Assistive technology can be [low-tech or high-tech]:
    - **[High-tech devices]**: voice synthesizers, braille readers, switch activated toys, computers and iPads
    - **[Low-tech tools]**: special handles on utensils and paintbrushes, pillows and bolsters, spoons with short handles, picture communication systems

- Assistive technology can help a child to:
  - [Participate more actively] in family, school and community activities
  - [Play successfully] with toys and other children
  - [Communicate] his or her needs and ideas
  - [Make choices]
  - [Move independently]
  - [Participate in age-appropriate activities]

4. Poor Reading Habits and Handwriting D.desc:
- **[Poor Reading Habits]**:
  - Children with reading difficulties often have poor reading habits that teachers should be aware of when watching students read

- Behaviours exhibited by children with poor reading habits (Gargiulo, 2004):
  - **[Tension movements]**: frowning, fidgeting, using a high-pitched tone of voice
  - **[Insecurity]**: refusing to read, crying, attempting to distract the teacher
  - **[Loses place]**: losing place frequently
  - **[Lateral head movements]**: jerking head
  - **[Holds material close]**: deviating extremely from 15 to 18 inches

- **[Handwriting Difficulties]**:
  - Handwriting refers to the [actual motor activity] involved in writing
  - Children with learning disabilities [lag behind] their nondisabled peers
  - Individuals with learning disabilities use [less complex sentence structure], incorporate fewer ideas, produce poorly organized paragraphs, and write less complex stories

- **[Dysgraphia]**:
  - Dysgraphia is the learning disability associated with [written expression], entailing writing skills that fall substantially below expected levels
  - It is the [inability to perform motor movement], in other words, extremely poor handwriting
  - It is associated with a [neurological dysfunction]

- Students' handwriting problems can arise from:
  - A lack of [fine motor coordination]
  - [Failure to attend to task]
  - Inability to [perceive and/or remember visual images] accurately
  - [Inadequate handwriting instruction] in the classroom

2. Academic Achievement and Reading Defi.desc:
- **[Academic Achievement Deficits]**:
  - During elementary school years, a [discrepancy between ability and achievement] begins to emerge
  - Students seem to have strengths similar to their peers but their [rate of learning is unexpectedly slower]
  - Problems usually persist from primary grades through the end of formal schooling
  - Academic deficits normally fall into: [reading, mathematics, and written expression]

- **[Reading Deficits]**:
  - Reading difficulties are observed among students with learning disabilities [more than any other problem area]
  - It is estimated that as many as [90%] of students with learning disabilities have reading difficulties
  - This problem is related to deficient language skills, especially [phonological awareness]—the ability to understand that speech flow can be broken into smaller sound units
  - Problems with phonology are at the [heart of many reading difficulties]
  - Reading disabilities may be more prevalent in [English-speaking countries] than in some other countries

- Problems that may prevent a child with learning disabilities from learning to read (Kirk, Gallagher, & Anastasiow, 2003):
  - [Faulty auditory perception] without hearing impairment
  - [Slow auditory or visual processing]
  - Inability to [distinguish or separate the sounds] of spoken words
  - [Lack of knowledge of the purpose] of reading
  - [Failure to attend to critical aspects] of the word, sentence, or paragraph
  - [Failure to understand that letters represent units] of speech

- According to Torgeson and Wagner (1998):
  - The most severe reading problems lie at the [word, rather than the text, level] of processing
  - The most common cognitive limitation involves a dysfunction in the [awareness of the phonological structure] of words in oral language

3. Oral Reading Difficulties and Word Re.desc:
- **[Oral Reading Difficulties]**:
  - Many students with learning disabilities have difficulties with [reading fluency]—the rate of accurate reading (correct words per minute)
  - Students may read aloud in a [word-by-word manner] without appropriate inflection or rhythm
  - Students often [dread being asked to read in class]

- Common oral reading problems (Salvia & Ysseldyke, 1998):
  - **[Omissions]**: the student skips individual words or groups of words
  - **[Insertion]**: the student inserts one or more words into the sentence being orally read
  - **[Substitution]**: the student replaces one or more words by one or more meaningful words
  - **[Gross mispronunciation]**: pronunciation bears little resemblance to proper pronunciation
  - **[Hesitation]**: the student hesitates for two or more seconds before pronouncing a word
  - **[Inversion]**: the student changes the order of words appearing in a sentence
  - **[Disregard of punctuation]**: the student fails to observe punctuation

- **[Problems with Word Recognition]**:
  - Students often have difficulties with word recognition, which relates to [sight vocabulary]
  - The more exposure a student has to specific words, the more readily he or she [recognizes those words]

- Important word analysis skills:
  - The ability to [associate sounds with letters] and letter combinations (phonic analysis)
  - Immediately [recognizing and remembering words] (sight-word reading)
  - [Using surrounding text] to help figure out a specific word (using context)

- Common word recognition errors (Gargiulo, 2004):
  - **[Omissions]**: omitting a word (Tom saw [a] cat)
  - **[Insertions]**: inserting words (The dog ran [fast] after the cat)
  - **[Substitutions]**: reversing letters in a word (no for on, was for saw)
  - **[Mispronunciations]**: (Mister for miser)
  - **[Transpositions]**: reading words in wrong order (She away ran instead of she ran away)
  - **[Unknown words]**: hesitating for 5 seconds at words they cannot pronounce
  - **[Slow choppy reading]**: not recognizing words quickly enough

1. Meaning of Observation.desc:
- Observation is an activity of a living being, such as a human, which is necessary in order to [receive knowledge of the world or about the environment through the senses], which often later involves the recording of data via the use of scientific instruments.

- The term may also refer to any [data collected] during this activity.

- It is the act of [regarding attentively and studying facts and occurrences], gathering data through analyzing, measuring, and drawing conclusions, with the purpose of applying the observed information to theoretical assumptions.

- Observation as a [scientific method] in the acquisition of knowledge began in classical antiquity; in modern science and medicine its greatest application is facilitated by modern technology.

- Observation is one of the [components of the research process].

anecdotal records: most common, short stories in past tense about child's engagement
anecdotal records record strengths, interests, development, needs

4. Types of Observation (Classroom-Based.desc:
- It is not considered best practice to stick to one type of observational method. Using differing types provides for a [better ability to assess the child's overall development].

- **[Individual & Group Learning Stories]**:
  - These documents are the more commonly used form of observation and provide [documented evidence of the child's learning] either individually or within a group
  - They help to demonstrate the [progress of a child] in their learning journey as well as assists educators to plan and extend on their learning
  - They include [pictures] to further demonstrate the learning occurring

- **[Jottings]**:
  - Usually [short details of significant events, behaviours or conversations]
  - A brief paragraph focusing on significant behaviours and information regarding the child
  - It is a useful method to use in conjunction with [photos and work samples]

- **[Event Samples]**:
  - This is mostly used when [focusing on a particular thing] such as behaviour (e.g., how often a child bites, or throws a tantrum)
  - Helps to establish such things as [possible trigger, how often the behaviour takes place and the type of environment] where it occurs
  - Provide evidence to show whether incidents are [continuing, escalating or have slowed or ceased]

- **[Photo Observations]**:
  - This is a [non-written form] of observation that may or may not be accompanied with a written form
  - It is a perfect [visual tool to capture a child at play], or practicing a skill or during social skills and interactions
  - Can clearly show the [level a child is at]. A good photo observation of cutting can clearly show the control, the grip, and ability

- **[Anecdotal Records]**:
  - This would probably be the [most common form] used if you were to look through a child's observations
  - It is basically a [short story that captures the child engaging in play] and the experience, written in past tense
  - Starts right at the beginning when the child begins the experience and ends when the child stops participating
  - It is a useful method to record events or actions based on the child's [strengths, interests, development and needs]

- **[Running Records]**:
  - This type is often described as [detailed observation] as what occurred is what is written
  - You write [exactly what is being seen] and a good running record offers loads of detailed information
  - Written in [present tense]
  - This method is considered useful in recording any [areas of development], note down child's interests as they occur and provides a holistic approach to observing a child

- **[Time Samples]**:
  - Good for working in conjunction with event samples in relating to [monitoring a specific behaviour]
  - They provide information on [what a child is doing at regular intervals] (e.g., every 15 minutes during the morning routine)
  - They are a good observational method to look at a child's [overall participation] within the learning environment and to observe a child's overall development in a range of contexts by capturing them at various regular intervals

- **[Sociograms]**:
  - This is perhaps the [most specific type] of observational method and possibly the least relevant in the whole early childhood setting as it is more geared to just the preschool children to [observe patterns of friendships]
  - This method is able to specify which child needs [more support in making friends] as a chart is created to monitor and point to their friendship references

3. Types of Observation (Research-Based.desc:
- **[Controlled Observation]**:
  - Here [observer and observed or subject are both controlled]
  - For systematic data collection, [control is imposed on both] for accuracy and precision
  - When observation is [pre-planned and definite], then it is termed as controlled observation
  - Mechanical devices are used for precision and standardization

  - Elements of controlled observation:
    - [Observational plan]
    - [Observational schedule]
    - [Mechanical appliances] like camera, maps, films, video, tape recorder
    - [Team of observers]
    - [Socio Matric Scale]

- **[Uncontrolled Observation]**:
  - Takes place in a [natural setting] without the influence of external or outside control
  - The observer [does not plan in advance] but this is related to day-to-day happenings and socio-cultural problems
  - It studies some of our [life situations]

- **[Structured and Unstructured Observation]**:
  - **[Structured Observation]**: careful information are recorded in a [standardized way]. It is a planned observation that follows certain patterns, rules and designs for the purpose of what, how and when to observe
  - **[Unstructured Observation]**: opposite to structured. This is [not systematic and unplanned]. A researcher does not set a plan in advance but gets information freely. There are [no rules to follow]

- **[General or Layman Observation]**:
  - Made by people in [day-to-day happenings]
  - They see many things daily but there is [no objectivity] of their study
  - Example: a person seeing children playing in a garden is general observation

- **[Scientific Observation]**:
  - Based on some [scientific rules and deliberate thinking]
  - The observer must know what to observe
  - He has [proper planning, objectivity, hypothesis and observation schedule] in his study
  - Scientific observation is [reliable and more standardized] than general observation

ex: participant observation: observer participates in group activities

participant observation demerits: emotional attachment, short time limitation, cannot cover wide area

non-participant observation merits: objectivity maintained, accuracy, secondary relationship, smooth research
non-participant observation demerits: no full knowledge, cannot understand whole phenomena, no deep insight
2. Types of Observation (Research-Based).desc:
- The types of observation can be grouped into two: those that pertain to [research] and those that pertain to [classroom only].

- **[Participant Observation]**:
  - First introduced by Prof. Edward Winder Man
  - The observer [participates himself] in the activities of the group and notes the situation
  - He willingly mixes with the group and performs his activities as an observer, not merely a participator who criticizes the situation
  - The best philosophy is that we [watch the phenomena, not to ask]
  - The actual behavior of the group can be observed only by participant observation

  - **[Merits]**:
    - The observer is personally involved and [shares feelings and prejudices]
    - He participates and gets [insight into the behavior] of the group
    - It motivates and stimulates [mutual relationship] between observer and observed
    - He can get more information with [accuracy and precision]
    - Information is recorded [in front of the group people]

  - **[Demerits]**:
    - The observer may develop [emotional attachment] to the group, losing objectivity
    - Cannot observe a certain phenomenon in a [short time]
    - Cannot [cover a wide area] through this method

- **[Non-Participant Observation]**:
  - The observer has a [lack of participation] in group activities
  - He either watches from a distance or participates in the group but [never in its activities]
  - He only sits in the group but [does not have interest] in the process

  - **[Merits]**:
    - The observer never attaches to the group, so [objectivity is maintained]
    - Less emotional involvement leads to [accuracy and greater objectivity]
    - Observer has [secondary relationship] with the group, so information is collected entirely
    - The research runs [very smooth]

  - **[Demerits]**:
    - Does not have [full knowledge] about group activities
    - Cannot [understand the whole phenomena]
    - Cannot get [real and deep insight] into the phenomena

1. What is a School Inventory?.desc:
- An [itemized catalog or list] of tangible goods or property, or the tangible attributes or qualities.

- It refers to the [value of materials and goods held by an organization]:
  - (1) to support production (raw materials, subassemblies, work in process)
  - (2) for support activities (repair, maintenance, consumables)
  - (3) for sale or customer service (merchandise, finished goods, spare parts)

- Inventory is often the [largest item in the current assets category], and must be accurately counted and valued at the end of each accounting period to determine a company's profit or loss.

- Organizations whose inventory items have large unit cost generally keep a [day-to-day record of changes in inventory] (called perpetual inventory method) to ensure accurate and ongoing control.

- There are various [school inventories] that are used either to store and record academic data or even items present within a particular school area.

- **[Specific samples of school inventories]**:
  - **[Equipment inventories]**: used to record all the [physical properties] present within the vicinity of the school's location. Most used by the maintenance and security department.
  - **[School data inventory]**: created to assure that [data resources needed by students are organized] and can be used right away when needed. The library and registrar's office can fully benefit from this.
  - **[School bus inventory]**: used to identify the [current condition of the school buses] owned by the academic institution. This may also include the maintenance dates and other items related to the vehicle's usage.

- **[How to Create an Inventory for School]**:
  - [Be specific] with the kind of school inventory you will create so you are fully aware of its purpose
  - [Know the entities] for which the school inventory is being made
  - [Open an inventory template] that can help you format a specific school inventory accordingly
  - [List down the items] that you need to either record, list down or keep, depending on the usage
  - Just like in asset inventory examples, [identify the description and quantity] of the items that you will place in the document

2. Challenges Faced by Inventory Manager.desc:
- **[Textbooks]**:
  - Keeping track of potentially [thousands of textbooks] issued to students is often a logistical nightmare
  - A quick system for [checking books in and out] at the beginning and end of a school year or semester will help you keep a better track

- **[Manual tracking of data]**:
  - Many school districts still rely on [written paper records] for tracking school inventory
  - These records are subject to quite a bit of [human error] and can be easily lost or improperly sorted
  - This information is [difficult to collate and analyze]
  - An [electronic system of asset management] is more convenient, accurate, and easy to examine for inconsistencies, trends, or problems

- **[Theft]**:
  - All too often, school system equipment is [stolen by students or employees]
  - Better tracking for school inventory can help school systems more quickly [identify potential theft problems]

- **[Cross-school and district transfers]**:
  - When school equipment is [transferred from one district to another], it can get lost in the move or packed away and never used

- **[Obsolescence and lifecycle management]**:
  - Computers and other equipment may be [replaced by newer models], but the current models may still have some life in them and could be used in administrative or office settings
  - Many obsolete electronics are [shelved in warehouses and never disposed of properly]
  - By keeping better track of obsolete equipment, schools can better direct resources and make use of obsolete, but still functional equipment

2. Types of Observation Checklists.desc:
- There are [three basic types] of observation checklists.

- **[Teacher Observation Checklist]**:
  - Used by school administrators to [evaluate the performance of teachers] to ensure that teaching practices are effective and meet school standards.

- **[Classroom Observation Checklist]**:
  - A tool used by school administrators to ensure that a [positive and conducive learning environment] is provided for the students.

- **[Student Observation Checklist]**:
  - Used by teachers to perform an individual or group evaluation on students regarding their [behaviors and interactions within the classroom].
  - Observation checklists allow teachers to [examine and adjust their teaching practices] to cater to the learning needs of their students and determine ways to correct disruptive behaviors.

ex: list of things an observer looks at during class observation

1. What is an Observation Checklist?.desc:
- An observation checklist is a [list of things that an observer is going to look at] when observing a class. This list may have been prepared by the observer or the teacher or both.

- Observation checklists not only give an observer a [structure and framework] for an observation, but also serve as a [contract of understanding] with the teacher, who may as a result be more comfortable, and will get specific feedback on aspects of the class.

- An observation checklist is a [set of questions that evaluate the performance and behavior] of teachers and students in a classroom setting.

- Observation checklists assist an observer to [identify skill gaps and problem areas] to further improve teaching strategies, classroom settings, and student learning development.

ex: academic behaviour: working habits, organizational skills
academic behaviour: attentiveness to instructions and tasks
social behaviour: interaction with peers and adults
social behaviour: friendliness, respect, appropriate interaction
general behaviour: disruptive behaviors, attention span, activity level
3. Key Areas to Observe in Student Obser.desc:
- **[Academic / Instructional Behaviour]**:
  - This checks for the student's [working habits and organizational skills]
  - Questions include: Does the student [work carefully and neatly]? Do they [follow along with instructions and tasks]? Is he/she [attentive to instructions and tasks]?

- **[Social Behaviour]**:
  - This refers to the interaction of the student with the people they're surrounded with, within the school (including [peers and adults])
  - Questions include: Is the student [friendly and respectful] towards adults and his/her peers? Do they [interact appropriately] with peers in an academic setting?

- **[General Behaviour and Conduct]**:
  - This area pertains to the student's [overall behavior]
  - Questions include: Does the student [engage in disruptive behaviors] in class? How is his/her [attention span]? What about his/her [activity level]?

ex: reliable data: active watching produces more reliable results
direct practice: students understand requirements, teachers identify skill gaps

can take place in classroom: alleviates fears, no travel costs

5. Benefits of Using an Observation Chec.desc:
- **[Reliable Data]**:
  - As the assessor is actively watching and recording the actions of the subject, the results can be deemed to be [more reliable].

- **[Direct Practice of an Action]**:
  - With the physical demonstration of the desired task, the student is immediately immersed in the actual work, helping them to [understand the teacher's requirements]. It also helps the teacher [identify any skills gaps] which need to be addressed.

- **[Immediate Results]**:
  - As the assessment takes place in real time with the teacher involved, results are available [immediately]. So the teacher doesn't have to wait endlessly for examination results.

- **[Can Take Place in the Classroom]**:
  - Unlike some assessment techniques, observational assessment can take place [within the classroom itself]. This helps alleviate fears of an unknown environment and benefits teachers who do not have additional travel costs.

- **[Ideal for Marking Project Work Where Team Activities are Undertaken]**:
  - Observational assessment can easily allow teachers to [mark multiple students who are working on group tasks] at the same time, giving a truer comparison than marking subjectively after the event.

- **[Versatility]**:
  - Checklists offer a [simple evaluation method] that you can use repeatedly. When you make your own behaviour checklist, you can [customize the list] to meet your specific objectives. You can adapt the original checklist as your evaluation needs change. This type of assessment is usually quick to complete since you only need to check whether or not the child exhibits the behavior.

- **[Specific Traits]**:
  - A checklist allows you to evaluate [specific traits in a child's behavior]. Instead of general observation, you can drill down to capture exactly what the child is doing and focus on those particular behaviors or traits for a targeted assessment. Because the checklist spells out exactly what you're looking for, many different people can administer the evaluation with [similar results].

4. Steps for Developing an Observation C.desc:
- **[Step 1: Focus on the checklist task]**
  - [Define the content area] of interest
  - [Define the checklist's intended uses]
  - [Reflect on and draw upon] pertinent training and experience
  - [Study the relevant literature]
  - [Engage and have conversations] with experts in the content area
  - [Clarify and justify the criteria] to be met by the checklist (e.g., pertinence, comprehensiveness, clarity, concreteness, ease of use, parsimony, applicability, and fairness)

- **[Step 2: Make a candidate list of checkpoints]**
  - [List descriptors] for well-established criteria of merit
  - [Briefly define] each of the initial checkpoints
  - [Add descriptors] for checkpoints needed to round out a definition of merit
  - [Provide definitions] for each of the added descriptors

- **[Step 3: Classify and sort the checkpoints]**
  - [Write each descriptor and definition] on a separate 4" × 6" card
  - [Sort the cards] in search of categories
  - [Identify the main candidate categories] and label each category

- **[Step 4: Define and flesh out the categories]**
  - [Define each category] and its key concepts and terms
  - [Write a rationale] for each category
  - [Present relevant warnings] about being overzealous in applying the checkpoint
  - [Review the checkpoints] in each category for inclusiveness, clarity, and parsimony
  - [Add, subtract, and rewrite checkpoints] as appropriate

- **[Step 5: Determine the order of categories]**
  - [Decide if order is an important consideration] regarding the intended uses
  - If so, [write a rationale] for the preferred order
  - [Provide an ordering] of the categories

- **[Step 6: Obtain initial reviews of the checklist]**
  - [Prepare a review version] of the checklist
  - [Engage potential users] to review and critique the checklist
  - [Interview the critics] to gain an in-depth understanding of their concerns and suggestions
  - [List the issues] in need of attention

- **[Step 7: Revise the checklist content]**
  - [Examine and decide] how to address the identified issues
  - [Rewrite the checklist content]

- **[Looking for data in the right places]**:

- **[Observational Data]**:
  - Teachers learn to [watch their students]. Observe behaviors while interacting with the student, when they are working on assignments by themselves or on a group assignment
  - Each situation results in [unique information] about each student
  - Questions to consider:

    - What is the behavior when students [talk with the teacher] or [present to the class]?

  - Observational data can be difficult to collect with consistency. It may be beneficial to have [another person spend time observing] (e.g., a trained teaching assistant)

5. Benefits of the Learning Story Approa.desc:
- **[Encouraging Involvement]**:
  - Stories are generally more [engaging and interesting] to read than more objective accounts of observations
  - Using narrative and photographs, which are [emotionally appealing and affirming], learning stories can act as a 'conscription device' inviting families and children to participate in assessment practices
  - Learning stories allow families a [window into the practices and purposes] of the educational setting, and draw attention to activities which lead to the development of learning dispositions

- **[Ability to Account for the Complexity of Learning]**:
  - Learning stories encourage [detailed observations and analysis] of learners, seeking connections and affirming complexity as an integral part of learning
  - By providing [rich descriptions], learning stories convey the intensity and complexity of events
  - They also provide description of the [environment and teaching interactions] that accompany and support the learning
  - Stories might say as much about [teaching as they do about learning], providing a source of validation for teaching

- **[Generative of Multiple Meanings and Interpretations]**:
  - Learning stories can have various meanings depending on [teachers', children's, families' and community values]
  - They recognize and value the ways in which teachers' lenses are shaped by their own [realities, histories and cultures]
  - By adding other perspectives, learning stories generate [diverse interpretations and ongoing possibilities] for sharing, negotiating, revisiting, developing and changing meanings
  - Such diversity makes space for the [uncertainty that should accompany the complexity of learning]
  - Integration of different voices addresses issues of [objectivity or validity], providing more robust analysis

- **[Enabling Children's Voices]**:
  - Learning stories have the potential to develop children's [metacognition], helping them to think about their knowledge, skills and learning
  - Inviting children to make their own self-assessments opens spaces for [children's voices] and for crucial connections between their everyday realities and the curriculum

- **[Constructing Competence]**:
  - Learning stories are congruent with this aim in both supporting children's [sense of competence] and helping to construct competencies
  - They aim to recognise and strengthen children's [learning dispositions], and to create affirming stories that identify children as strong and capable in various roles and contexts
  - A portfolio collection of learning stories [celebrates the child as a competent individual] and acknowledges the child's strengths

- **[Enabling Continuity]**:
  - Learning stories can also document [interconnections between stories], and aspects of children's learning, by making links backwards (to previous events) and forwards (to potential plans)
  - This [recognizes learning as continuous] and open to development
  - Learning stories support ongoing continuity in learning by [identifying connections] between and across interests and strengths, and by providing materials to take learning in new directions

- **[Supporting Transitions]**:
  - Research shows that negative impacts of transition to school can be overcome when [effective communication channels] are established between schools and other agencies that enable assessment information to be shared and discussed
  - This sharing and use of assessment information [highlights learning and progress over time], and enhances the links between learning that has taken place at one setting and that which occurs at school
  - It helps children see themselves as [competent, confident learners], making transition more likely to be successful
  - Assessment information [empowers children with a strong learner identity] as they enter school and, in connecting knowledge of home and the educational setting with that of school, fosters children's sense of belonging and engagement

4. How to Use the Learning Story Approac.desc:
- **[See yourself as a learner]**:
  - Undertake a [learning journey together with the child]. This means you use assessment to learn more about the child, their knowledge and strengths, and their unique ways of approaching learning

- **[Focus on getting to know children well through assessment]**:

- **[Remember all three components of a learning story]**:
  - The [narrative, the analysis and the planning]. [Notice, recognize and respond]
  - First, you [notice] a lot of things as you work with children
  - You [recognize] some of that as learning
  - Finally, you [respond] to a selection of what you recognize

- **[Write when you are emotionally invested]**:
  - Write when you [notice things that matter to you], rather than planning to write for an allocated set of children or following a schedule
  - When teachers have an [emotional connection and responsive engagements] with children's learning, then planning will be more meaningful

- **[Write stories as close as possible to the learning event time]** :
  - This is in order to be [most responsive to children's learning], and also to share stories with all teachers so that every teacher can support this learning

1. What is Learning Story Approach?.desc:
- Learning story approach refers to a process in which learning is [structured around a narrative or story] as a means of 'sense making'.

- It involves the use of [personal stories and anecdotes] to engage learners and share knowledge.

- Stories are everywhere in human life and can be termed [narrative, case study, life history, myth, anecdote, legend, scenario, illustration or example, storytelling and/or critical incident].

- 'Stories' can be 'told' in many ways – [spoken, written, filmed, mimed, acted, presented as cartoons and/or as new media formats] (Moon 2010).

- The art of storytelling has always been an [essential part of being human]. Before the advent of the written word, storytellers were viewed as special members of society.

- Storytelling was viewed as a [vocation] and storytellers were required to develop skills in the appropriate use of language, communicating with impact, insight and sensitivity.

- Storytellers were required to develop their [memory and visualization skills] and use these skills to paint a picture in the mind of their audience, a picture through which they might simplify complex issues.

- In some communities, the appointment of the leader was ["based largely on their proficiency as a storyteller"] (Parkin 1998).

- Although educators have always used storytelling, until recently these stories often occurred [spontaneously] and were considered too lightweight to be integral to learning and teaching activities.

- With the growing awareness of the need for [reflection within learning], and the recognition that meaningful links need to be created between theory and practice, the potential for learning through storytelling is beginning to be recognised.

- Storytelling is now being used in a [reflective, creative and formalised manner] to facilitate learning within the realms of science, medical training, business schools, executive education, English, Languages, Arts and Drama.

- Anthropologist, Peg Neuhauser, describes how stories allow a person to [feel, and see, the information, as well as factually understand it] because you 'hear' the information factually, visually and emotionally, it is more likely to be imprinted on your brain in a way that it sticks with you longer, with very little effort on your part (Neuhauser 1993, p.4).

2. Characteristics of Learning Story App.desc:
- **[Concretizing]**:
  - Stories help us to [make sense of what might otherwise be an abstract or complex subject] through the provision of links with tangible, or concrete, examples
  - For example, the story of the impact of the market upon healthcare can provide a concrete example of marketization within the public sector

- **[Assimilation]**:
  - Learning is a constant process of [integrating new information with current information and cognitive structures] (cf. 'Scaffolding' and Piaget's stages of learning)
  - The use of a story, particularly a well-known story, or one to which everyone can relate, can be an effective way of [introducing a new topic] to learners, or enabling them to see earlier information through a new perspective
  - For example, the scene from Alice in Wonderland where Alice meets the Cheshire Cat can take on a new perspective if introduced within the framework of 'goal setting'

- **[Structurizing]**:
  - The use of stories in teaching can support students in [applying the concepts they have been taught to use in other situations], not directly related to the initial context (Parkin 1998)
  - Simons (1984) explored the power of story for teaching complex subjects such as electricity. He tested 61 students with 40-item, multiple choice tests
  - The results found that teaching using stories led to [improved performance in all students], with those who learnt from story having a more tangible grasp of the topic, and of the relationship between concepts

6. Advantages of Socio-Cultural Approach.desc:
- **[You can leverage multiple experts' knowledge]**:
  - Students learn from more than just the teacher
  - If learning through social interactions is ideal, then we would value [bringing experts and practitioners into the classroom]
  - Example: bring in a firefighter to teach about fire safety or a soldier to speak on Remembrance Day

- **[It can be inclusive of multiple cultural perspectives]**:
  - Sociocultural theorists understand people of different cultures [learn in different ways]
  - Teachers should learn what their students' cultural perspectives are and [include them in teaching]
  - If a student comes from a storytelling culture, bring their stories into learning scenarios
  - This helps not only minority cultures but helps all children [learn to respect cultural difference]

- **[Differentiation is embedded into the theory]**:
  - Differentiation is a [student-centered pedagogical concept] that highlights changing up teaching for each student
  - Some students need easier tasks, some need harder tasks
  - Theories like behaviorism fail to differentiate
  - Sociocultural theory is [very strong on differentiation] due to the idea of ZPD

7. Weaknesses of Socio-Cultural Approach.desc:
- **[It doesn't take into account children's emotions]**:
  - The theory of humanism does a much better job of taking into account children's emotions
  - Socioculturalists are [almost completely silent on the idea that children's emotions impact their learning]
  - This is a big oversight

- **[Sociocultural classrooms can be extremely noisy]**:
  - Social learning environments can become [so noisy and hard to manage] that you need to be a very effective educator
  - You need to teach students [self-regulation] and ensure they stay on task and keep voices down so they don't distract others

- **[Shy, timid or introverted students may struggle]**:
  - Many students who are [shy or prefer learning in isolation] have a lot of trouble in social learning environments
  - Introverted persons can get very exhausted in these environments and might learn much more effectively in silence and solitude

- **[Students with sensory challenges find it hard]**:
  - Many students struggle with the [high-energy, noisy and active environment] of a sociocultural classroom
  - Students with [autism] can really be set off by these challenging environments

- **[Differentiating learning for ZPD is very time consuming with big groups]**:
  - As a teacher in social learning spaces, it can be hard to [differentiate content so that students are all learning within their ZPD]
  - To differentiate content for 30 students is unrealistic. The best that can be achieved is splitting students into table groups

2. Features or Characteristics of Socio-.desc:
- The sociocultural approach to learning is identified with [two unique features].

- **[Learning is influenced by Social Interactions]**:
  - This is the most important concept: we learn through [social interactions]
  - This concept differentiates itself significantly from Piaget's 'cognitive-constructivist' ideas
  - Piaget saw children as ['lone scientists'] who learn by exploring their environment and absorbing information
  - Sociocultural theorists see learning as [fundamentally shaped through interactions between children and the adults] in their environment, starting from early childhood and continuing to adulthood
  - **[Cognitive-Constructivist Theory]** (Piaget, Froebel, Montessori): Parents and teachers stand back and let children learn in solitude
  - **[Sociocultural Theory]** (Vygotsky, Bruner, Rogoff): Parents and teachers usually interact with children to guide their learning
  - There is a [bigger role of the teacher or parent] in sociocultural theory than perhaps any other educational theory

- **[Learning is influenced by Culture]**:
  - The logical extension is that children learn [differently depending on their social environments]
  - Example: Comparing a child learning in a traditional indigenous Ghanaian community vs. a contemporary Western classroom
    - Indigenous children learn on the job (hunting, chopping firewood, cooking)
    - Western children learn in classrooms reading books
  - These children develop [different skills based on their cultural environment]
  - This suggests that Piaget's universal stages of cognitive development may be inaccurate, as children may develop [very differently around the world]

1. Explanation of Socio-Cultural Approac.desc:
- Vygotsky's sociocultural theory of learning explains that learning occurs during [social interactions between individuals]. It is one of the dominant theories of education today.

- It believes learning happens [first through social interaction and second through individual internalization] of social behaviours.

- **[Definitions]**:
  - Nagel (2012, p. 83) argues that sociocultural theory ["reflects the view that learning and development is not just a process of increased mental sophistication but is also mediated through social and cultural interactions."]
  - Bates (2019, p. 19) argues that through sociocultural theory, ["knowledge and interactions are constructed through social interactions with family, friends, teachers and peers."]
  - Leonard (2002, p. 178) states: ["sociocultural theory focuses on the causal relationship between social interaction and individual cognitive development."]

4. Principles for Using Socio-Cultural A.desc:
- **[Table Group Layouts are used to Support Social Interaction]**:
  - By placing students into table groups, they are placed in situations where they can [look at one another]
  - This encourages them to [discuss and interact while learning]. Commonly found in contemporary classrooms, particularly in early years

- **[Guests are invited into the Classroom]**:
  - Inviting guests encourages [social interactions between learners and experts in the 'real world']
  - Teachers often bring in key experts on topics they're learning (called 'incursions') to help children learn through interactions with real-life experts

- **[Parent-Teacher Collaborations bring Culture into the Classroom]**:
  - Teachers are encouraged to [interact with and collaborate with parents] on their teaching
  - By consulting with parents, teachers can learn more about their students' cultures and bring those [desirable cultural values and culturally-specific ways of learning] into the classroom

- **[Play-Based Learning is Common in Early Childhood Centers]**:
  - Play-based learning is one of the most [effective forms of learning]
  - Through play, children use language to interact with one another and help move each other through their [Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)]
  - Often, peers in play situations are the best ['more knowledgeable others'] who teach their friends new ways of thinking and doing tasks

5. How to Use Socio-Cultural Approach in.desc:
- **[Peer-to-Peer]**:
  - Students can work in [pairs or in small groups], giving opportunity to interact with each other, share innovative ideas, enhance learning, thoughts, feelings, and points of view
  - Classroom environment should be [comforting]. Students should feel free to ask questions and put forward ideas
  - Teacher's role should be [identifying the goals of student needs] and tasks should be skillful. Teachers should provide higher level of support
  - Example: teachers may give worksheets on cultural facts of different countries (currency like Dollar, Pound, Euro)

- **[Face-to-Face Discussion]**:
  - Breaks down the [nervousness or anxiety] of students
  - Learners can gain more [confidence] and become eligible to gather knowledge in a befitting manner
  - Teachers should set meetings weekly or monthly and have [friendly conversation]
  - Face-to-face conversation creates effectiveness, allowing students to share personal feelings, anxiety, eagerness, dreams, difficulties
  - Teachers should have [patience] and listen carefully to find out problems and introduce sociocultural perspective

- **[Family or Parents]**:
  - Family plays a [vital role] in teaching sociocultural facts to a child
  - Parents can be invited into the classroom to assist students, as many students are more [comfortable with their parents] than a teacher
  - This provides a [safe and secure environment] for students in their learning process
  - Example: students and parents may have a movie time where students learn about sociocultural factors
  - Use family members as [more competent individuals] to assist students, thereby increasing their ZPD

- **[Community]**:
  - Community learning is another way of learning sociocultural sites very well
  - By bringing community members into the classroom, students learn there is a [society or community larger than their family]
  - Cognitive development is also a [community of practice], where a learner learns how to communicate or interact with others (Mason, 2007, p. 2)
  - Example: community people may introduce their own family, culture, lifestyle, interaction, origin
  - Use community members as [more competent individuals] to assist students, thereby increasing their ZPD

- **[Interactive Task]**:
  - Interactive tasks help students have better understanding about surroundings and sociocultural views
  - For L2 learning, [speaking and writing skills] are great activities. Sawin and Lapkin (2002) said speaking and writing are used as cognitive skills that mediate interaction, improve psychological factors, make students social, create interest, and standardize knowledge
  - [Pair work, role play, presentation] are considered interactive tasks
  - Interactive tasks help students know one's culture in a more detailed way
  - Teachers can conduct [interesting games, presentations] and evaluate L2 acquisition through criteria such as eye contact, pronunciation, body gesture

- **[Individual Work]**:
  - Vygotsky's theory talks about small groups, but there is also opportunity to incorporate individual work
  - Individual work can be used as [assessments]
  - Working alone shows the teacher [what the student actually knows] and how much actual development has increased
  - Example: [response paper, term paper, research work, individual presentation] are individual work that increase student's skills for the future

8. Summary of Session Three.desc:
- Lev Vygotsky, the proponent of the socio-cultural approach, is of the view that learning happens [first through social interaction and second through individual internalization of social behaviours].

- To be able to see these, certain features must manifest:
  - The learning must be [influenced by social interaction]
  - It must also be [influenced by culture]
  - These two ideas characterize the socio-cultural approach

- We also discussed the main [principles that guide the use of the socio-cultural approach] to learning:
  - [Table group layout is used to support social interaction]
  - [Guests should be invited into the classroom]
  - [Parents-teachers should collaborate] by bringing culture into the classroom
  - [Play-based learning must be adopted] since it helps in early childhood centers

- This approach comes with some [advantages and disadvantages]. We discussed some of them as:
  - **[Advantages]**:
    - You can leverage multiple experts' knowledge
    - It can be inclusive of multiple cultural perspectives
    - Differentiation is embedded into the theory
  - **[Disadvantages]**:
    - It doesn't take into account children's emotions
    - Sociocultural classrooms can be extremely noisy
    - Shy, timid or introverted students may struggle
    - Students with sensory challenges find it hard

3. Key Theoretical Concepts in the Socio.desc:
- **[Key Sociocultural Theorists]**:
  - **[Lev Vygotsky (1896-1934)]**: The most well-known sociocultural theorist. Developed key terms like ['Zone of Proximal Development'] and ['More Knowledgeable Other']. Wrote 'Thinking and Speech' (1934) and 'Mind in Society' (1978). His ideas gained popularity in the West in the 1980s.
  - **[Jerome Bruner (1915-2016)]**: Continued Vygotsky's argument that [language shapes thought]. Worked at Harvard and Oxford, examined how parents and children interact. In 1983 wrote 'Child's Talk: Learning to Use Language'. His most influential contribution today is the concept of ['scaffolding'].
  - **[Barbara Rogoff (1950- )]**: One of the most influential female developmental psychologists. Completed PhD exploring language development among the Tz'utujil Mayan people of Guatemala. Reinforced the idea that [language develops in unique cultural contexts]. Key contributions include ['cognitive apprenticeships'] and ['guided participation'].

- **[The Term 'Internalization']**:
  - One of the most important terms in sociocultural theory
  - We [internalize the knowledge that we observe, see and interact with]
  - The more we're exposed to a certain way of thinking, the more we [internalize those thought patterns]
  - This differs from Piaget's view, who didn't emphasize how we are influenced by others
  - **[Vygotsky's Internalization]**: Our ways of thinking are influenced by the thought patterns of those around us
  - **[Piaget's View]**: Our ways of thinking happen independently of the people around us
  - Example: Being born into a Christian family makes one more likely to believe in Jesus; being born into an atheist family makes one more likely to internalize atheist ideas
  - When writing about sociocultural theory, use the term ['Internalization'] to explain that we develop our ways of thinking by internalizing the thought patterns of others

1. Definition of 'Game' and Educational.desc:
- It refers to a [contest] in which players and opponents operate under rules to gain a specific objective (Cruickshank, 1977).

- **[Educational games]** are those activities we choose to include in lessons to [refresh minds and bodies of our students] according to approved rules which align with our lesson topics and teaching goals.

- **[Definition of video games]** (Kirriemuir and McFarlane, 2004):
  - Video games provide some [visual digital information or substance] to one or more players
  - Video games take some [input from the players]
  - Video games [process the input] according to a set of programmed game rules
  - Video games [alter the digital information] provided to the players

- Games that are generally played in electronic devices such as computers, game consoles and mobile gadgets are known as [digital games]. They are classified based on the platform:
  - [Computer-based video games]
  - [Console-based digital games] such as Sony Play Station, Nintendo (DS and 3DS), Nintendo-Wii, Microsoft Xbox
  - [Mobile devices based] such as smart phones, e-readers and other hand-held devices

- Each game can be classified into [single player games] and [multiplayer games]. Further these games are classified as:
  - [Action games]
  - [Adventure games]
  - [Strategy games]
  - [Process-oriented games] (Egenfeldt-Nielsen, Smith & Tosca, 2008)

5. Advantages of Using Games in the Clas.desc:
- **[Improved recall and retention]**:
  - Attitude plays an important role in how well students recall material
  - Classroom games encourage [creative thinking and problem-solving]
  - Games help students [recall information better], especially visual learners who like to see concepts come to life
  - Combine readings and lecturing with interactive classroom role-playing to [help cement the material]
  - Memorable and engaging experiences will [stick with students well beyond class]

- **[Development of problem-solving skills]**:
  - Good games help develop [essential problem-solving skills], especially important for children and teens
  - Games help students get [thinking and answering questions], not just reading and memorizing
  - Example: teaching geometry through real-world problem solving or labs with physical objects

- **[Instant feedback]**:
  - Classroom games improve student engagement and give [instant, valuable feedback] on how well students are really doing
  - Fun games [open up even the shyest of students] and get everyone involved

3. Characteristics of Educational Games.desc:
- **[Ease of use]**:
  - Students shouldn't have to sift through manuals and tutorials. Effective classroom games are [simple and to the point]
  - At the same time, they need to be [sufficiently challenging] so class time isn't wasted on trivial activities

- **[Engagement]**:
  - Consider distributing a [survey] to determine what type of activity students would like to do
  - Whatever game you settle on needs to actually be [enjoyable for most (if not all) students]

- **[Customization]**:
  - The best classroom games allow students to [learn the material while giving them creative freedom] to express themselves in their own unique ways

- **[Purpose]**:
  - Engaging classroom games have [meaningful and tangible goals] for students to complete
  - Games should [combine fun with deliberate and well-designed lesson plans]
