4. Principles for Using Games.desc:
- **[Have a concrete plan in mind]**:
  - Decide: Will students play every day or only on certain days? Before or after lectures? For how long? At home or in the classroom?
  - How you administer the game is up to you

- **[Decide on the right format]**:
  - With smartphones and handheld devices, [digital games] are very popular
  - If digital games won't work, use other in-class activities like [group labs, in-class presentations, Jeopardy-style assessments]
  - Consider if you'd like students to work [independently or collaboratively]
  - Make sure the format works for you, your students, and is easy to implement

- **[Assess effectiveness, gather feedback, and iterate]**:
  - Make sure games are actually [helping students master the content] and make progress
  - With educational digital games, it's easy if students have their own accounts with [built-in means of measuring progress] (e.g., XP points)
  - Students should have some [say in how they learn]. Seek feedback to ensure games are working well
  - If difficulty doesn't scale well, [split class into tiered groups] that match their proficiency level

- **[Embed the instructional game into the curriculum]**:
  - Games should be embedded in instructional programs that include [debriefing and feedback]
  - Best learning outcomes occur when a [three-step process] is followed:
    - (1) Teacher introduces the game and learning objectives
    - (2) Students play the game
    - (3) Teacher debriefs students on what they learned

- **[Games need to include instructional support]**:
  - Without instructional support, students learn how to play the game rather than learn [domain-specific knowledge]
  - Instructional support helps learners focus on [instructional information rather than game requirements]
  - Support features can include [elaborative feedback, pedagogical agents, and multi-modal information presentation] (Wouter, 2013; Ke, 2009; Hays, 2005)

- **[Ensure game objectives align with curriculum objectives]**:
  - Learning outcomes depend largely on how educationalists align [learning subject areas, learner characteristics, and game-based pedagogy] with the design of an instructional game
  - If game objectives match curriculum objectives, disconnects are avoided between game design and curricular goals (Schifter, 2013)
  - The more closely aligned curriculum goals and game goals, the more the [learning outcomes will match desired outcomes]

6. Disadvantages of Using Games in the C.desc:
- **[Doubt about whether students are actually benefiting]**:

  - They are unsure if the effort put into using games is worth the end result

7. Summary of Session Two.desc:
- In this session we looked at the meaning of demonstration activity and said it is an activity which combines [telling, showing and doing] for the benefits of an audience.

- The demonstration activity is used when one wants to teach a [practical skill or a process skill].

5. Advantages of Demonstration.desc:
- It [stimulates thinking] and the formation of concepts and generalizations.

- It [saves time and money] and as such it is more economical.

- It [trains students to be good observers].

- It is very effective as an [introduction to skill learning].

- It is [activity-oriented]. It thus directs and sustains interest in learning; this in turn helps learners understand what they are learning.

- Once learners understand why they go through demonstration, they can [retain the knowledge and skills acquired] and use them in real life situations. This transfer of knowledge and skills is very vital.

3. Principles/Guidelines Underlying Demo.desc:
- **[Task analysis]**:
  - Demonstration requires an [analysis of the objective] as this will serve as a guide in deciding which aspect of the demonstration to emphasize.

- **[Preparation of explanation materials]**:
  - Since a demonstration is a [dramatic performance], make sure that all the materials needed are available and that every piece is exactly where you want it to be when you require it.

- **[Rehearsal of the demonstration]**:
  - It is useful to give your demonstration a [trial run], if possible, to an audience which knows little or nothing about the subject. This will help you determine the clarity of your procedure and whether the equipment is in good working order.

- Prepare an [outline showing the main steps] in logical order.

- **[Prepare the environment]** by arranging the seats and items for the lesson in such a way that learners can see and hear well.

- **[Prepare a written material]** which may include mimeograph handout or a written summary. If shown before or during lesson, the students may pay little attention to the actual act of demonstration.

1. The Meaning of Demonstration Techniqu.desc:
- Demonstration is an activity which combines [telling, showing and doing] for the benefits of an audience.

- It has also been described as an [audio-visual explanation] which is used to emphasize an important point to a product, a process or an idea.

- In demonstration the principal performer is the [teacher] and the students his audience and the materials stage props.

- Demonstration is often used in [relation with other forms of techniques].

- The emphasis in demonstration is [learning by observing], and is often followed by doing.

- Drills and practice exercises are frequently provided to enable learners [perform the activity] after the teacher has demonstrated an activity.

4. Principles/Guidelines Underlying Demo.desc:
- **[Introduce the lesson]** by telling students the [objectives of the demonstration]. This is to motivate them to participate in the lesson.

- **[Give an overview]** of the entire demonstration. This is to allow the learners have an idea of what is to be learnt. Do not go into fine details. Keep your demonstration [simple and short].

- **[Performing should be done at a considerable pace]**. In order to help the student understand each step, make sure to [describe the action performed at each stage]. You can repeat the demonstration, or have one of the learners repeat it for others to observe.

- **[After the demonstration allow pupils to ask questions]**. In answering questions, refrain from deviating from the main points. Try to [stay on track] all the time.

- **[Summarise and conclude the demonstration]**. Give handouts to learners.

6. Disadvantages of Demonstration.desc:

- When classes are large, students will find it difficult to [hear and see the demonstration].

- It is difficult to [evaluate thoroughly student's understanding] during a demonstration.

- Active participation is reduced as the learners mainly act as [observers].

- It provides less opportunity for learners to [discover things or solve problems] on their own.

2. Instances When Demonstration Is Neces.desc:
- When there are [not enough instructional materials] for individuals or group study. In such situations it becomes necessary to demonstrate while learners formulate their conclusions.

- When [teaching a process or skill]. Through observation, students would be in the position to perform a skill. An example is the process involved in making a puppet or marionette.

- When the [equipment or materials to be used are expensive and delicate]. In order to avoid accidents or damaging the equipment and to save a cost of maintenance, a demonstration and guided practice is necessary.

- The method to be used to teach a lot of [practical skills or processes] especially when time available to teach a topic is limited.

4. Steps for Planning a Field Trip (Sche.desc:
- **[Plan your schedule for the day]**:
  - When do kids need to arrive at school?
  - When are you departing and coming back?
  - What activities will you do and for how long?
  - Will there be recreation or "free" time?
  - Do you need to bring any toys, tools, or other supplies?
  - Do your best to schedule everything perfectly with [few gaps] and avoid unexpected surprises

- **[Arrange supervision and volunteers]**:
  - For older, well-behaved students, you may not need extra supervision
  - Younger kids may need more supervision; check with your administrator to bring a [teacher's aide (or two)] along
  - Ask for parents to volunteer as [chaperones]
  - As a rule, you'll want [one adult for every 10 kids], depending on age, maturity, and behavior

- **[Decide who is allowed to go]**:
  - You may want to make the trip an [incentive] (e.g., require good behavior for a week before the trip)
  - If you do this, arrange for kids not allowed to attend (study hall, library trip) with adult supervision

- **[Tie the trip into your curriculum]**:
  - Have curriculum lessons [directly related to your field trip] in the days leading up to it
  - Have students do background reading and create in-class activities that tie in with the trip

- **[Have a communication plan in place]**:
  - Have a plan to communicate with adults and parents if something goes wrong (weather delay, flat tire, sickness, allergic reaction)
  - Contact parents of every student immediately via [SMS text messaging] in case of emergency

6. Problems with Field Trips.desc:
- **[Preparation time]**:
  - Field trips take [preparation] if teachers want to make them meaningful. They have to coordinate locations and transportation and create effective lesson plans.

- **[Missing other classes]**:
  - Students will be out of the school building, which means they will [miss other classes]—at least in JHS and SHS
  - If each core subject offers one field trip during a school year, students would be out for four days
  - School attendance policies may count these as excused absences, but any field trip reduces classroom hours

- **[Cost]**:
  - Field trips can be [expensive], and some students may not have the funds to attend
  - Organizers may ask parents to add a few dollars to help students in need
  - School boosters may need to host a fundraiser for more expensive trips
  - Teachers have to organize the collection of money and assigning of chaperones

- **[Student grouping]**:
  - Teachers need to spend time [creating student groups] that work for all students and ensuring chaperones are assigned accordingly

- **[Red tape]**:
  - Teachers will likely have to deal with [red tape] including permission slips, medical information, and emergency procedures. Schools typically require paperwork from teachers and students

- **[Discipline problems]**:
  - Students will be placed in a larger environment than the classroom. New surroundings could lead to [additional discipline problems]
  - Teachers leading small groups may not maintain control over every student, especially if the group is large
  - Teachers should go over rules before the trip, enforce them strictly, and create effective consequences for misbehavior

- **[Destination not meeting expectations]**:
  - The destination might not live up to the teacher's expectations. It might not be as interesting or the time to complete might be less than expected
  - Have a [contingency plan] in mind just in case

- **[Students not attending]**:
  - There may be students who, for one reason or another, will not attend the field trip
  - Teachers must leave lessons, usually [enrichment offerings] that mirror concepts being experienced on the field trip

  - Alternatively, have them [pack their own lunches]
  - Have some extra food on-hand in case a student forgets lunch

5. Benefits of Field Trips.desc:
- Field trips provide students with new opportunities for [learning through experience].

- Information is presented to students in a way that meets [different learning modalities]. Field trips provide students with the ability to learn by doing instead of just passively listening.

- Students are exposed to new experiences that, hopefully, [broaden their horizons]. This is especially helpful for students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

- Concepts that have already been learned in the classroom can be [reinforced]. Sometimes seeing information in a new way can make a big difference in student comprehension.

- Students are provided with [shared reference points] that teachers can then refer to and use in future lessons.

- There may be an opportunity to have [two or more disciplines use a field trip] as an enrichment activity (e.g., art museum combining art, social studies, and math). Several teachers can refer to things students saw for the remainder of the school year.

- Students and teachers can see each other in a [different light], helping to increase communication. Quiet students might really come alive on field trips.

- If parents are involved as chaperones, they can feel more [connected to the teacher and the lessons] being taught. They can get to know the teacher better and understand what teachers deal with daily.

- Standards in social studies and science require students to have [experiences related to concepts] in the discipline. Field trips help teachers meet these objectives.

1. Explanation of Field Trip.desc:
- The term ["field trip"] has been known for decades in many sectors and it is a common term used in worldwide schools.

- A field trip or [excursion] is a journey taken by a group of people to a place away from their usual environment.

- In education, field trips are defined as [visits to an area outside of the normal classroom], made by a teacher and students for purposes of firsthand observation.

- A field trip can be expressed in many terminologies. People call it [educational trips or school tours] in the UK and New Zealand, and school tours in the Philippines.

- Field trips are a popular method carried out for students to introduce to the [concepts, experiences, and ideas that cannot be given in a classroom environment].

- School tours can be considered as [short-term learning activities] providing students the opportunity to observe their chosen subjects outside of a classroom setting.

- Exploring other cultures and customs, getting to the motherland of languages, uncovering pristine nature and experiencing fascinating local life are striking demonstrations of educational school trips.

educational: improve language, explore culture, homestay, language lessons

2. Types of Field Trips.desc:
- These refer to [listed field trip ideas] that help to make the meaning of a field trip clearer.

- **[Sightseeing Field Trip]**:
  - Students are eager for sightseeing school trips, enchanting them by [appealing attractions] in their wish destination
  - Activities: admiring well-known attractions, exploring historic structures, discovering World Heritage Sites, unwinding on spectacular landscapes, freshening in front of scenic vista

- **[Educational Field Trip]**:
  - For students learning foreign languages, field trips are very important and helpful to [improve the language and explore the alluring indigenous culture]
  - Activities: joining immersive activities, staying at a local homestay, taking language lessons, visiting local markets

- **[Gardening and Farming Field Trip]**:
  - Interesting activity attracting lots of students' attention due to its [strangeness to their usual life]
  - Activities: discovering specialty farms, learning how vegetables are produced, exploring and trying traditional farming techniques

- **[Manufacturing Facility Field Trip]**:
  - Students can be guided to any factory where equipment, cars, tools, packaging or other things are made
  - The [mechanized facilities and assembly lines] are interesting for students to learn about the production process, how raw materials are utilized, and how workers make the final product

- **[Eco-adventure Field Trip]**:
  - Discovering the natural world is a highly important perspective in the educational sector
  - Activities: trekking through untouched natural beauties to inspect local plant life and wildlife animals, adding to local historical factors

- **[Business Educational Tour]**:
  - On business study trips, students will be delighted by the [bustling financial and business centers]
  - Business study trips help process business theories in the classroom into life as students explore great commercial organizations
  - Visiting a range of famed organizations and large corporations perfects business school trips

5. When to Choose the Use of the Role-Pl.desc:
- In choosing to use role-play, the topic must be such that there are at least [two or more characters] who will be required to play the varied roles.

- The [lesson objectives] are important. The teacher must find out if the technique would achieve the intended outcomes. Effective objectives often call for role-playing.

- **[Developmental stage of the class]**:

- Role-play situations should be [sufficiently familiar to the player] such that they can be expected to imagine how people might act in such situations.

1. Explanation of Role Play.desc:
- Role playing may be defined as a [structured activity] in which students assume or take the role of specific people or personalities in imaginary situations.

- Learners act the role or part of specific people in a [realistic way as possible].

- In role playing there are [no scripts] for actors indicating particular words or gestures they should use.

- Role playing activities are therefore [spontaneous] and it is not rehearsed or written.

- Learners receive a [problem situation and short description of character]. They take the role of the characters and make their own lines.

- Role play is often used to [practice interacting with other people].

- In short, role play is a [group problem in spontaneous enactment]. In role playing an attempt is made to make situations clear or solve a problem through unrehearsed dramatization.

6. Advantages of Role Play.desc:

- Role-play improves the [power of imagination] of students as they act out imaginary scenes and situations.

- Role-playing is conducive to [attitude change].

- It adds [variety to class work].

- This technique helps students through [emotion-laden situations]. It provides them with the opportunity to be in somebody else's shoes.

- Role-play develops [empathy and understanding].

- Also, it helps participants to gain [insight into their own behaviour] and how to deal with similar situations before experiencing them in future.

- It shows [different ways of handling a problem situation].

4. Steps in Designing/Conducting a Role-.desc:
- Teacher presents a [brief introduction] of the problem to be role-played and outlines the situation with learners' participation.

- Teacher [invites pupils to select role].

- [Select casts] from the volunteers and define role for them.

- [Non-players are prepared] to observe the role-play and to evaluate the performance.

- Allow pupils (cast) [ample time to prepare] for the role-playing. Be sure each knows exactly what she/he is going to do.

- Teacher [sets the stage] for the role-play e.g., displaying of needed materials.

- [Actual enactment] by role players. Roles are played for audience to watch. Players act in turns.

- [Class discussions] under the direction of the teacher (debriefing stage).

- **[Audience and Cast Discuss Together]**:
  - What happened?
  - Why did it happen?
  - Alternatives to what happened
  - Evaluation of the skills with which different roles were played
  - Suggestions about how to improve the performance

- **[Conclusion]**: The teacher summarizes the main points learned from the activity by providing chalkboard summary or posing oral questions during the debriefing stage.

3. Characteristics of Group Project in t.desc:
- You will likely encounter resistance when introducing group work. Students may complain or question the validity of your methods.

- To maximize success, [start slow and start early] in the semester. If you interweave small collaborative activities into every class, a group assignment later will seem less daunting.

- **[Creating]**:
  - A great way for students to demonstrate knowledge is to retrieve and synthesize information by collaborating with peers to [create something new]: a written piece, a song, a computer programme, code, a pamphlet, an infographic, or sample test questions

- **[Investigating]**:
  - Students can work together to [investigate a question and report findings] back to the class
  - Example: explore the debunked myth of learning styles. Students can find scholarly evidence to support or refute the claim, or collect personal experiences
  - Students help [generate course content] and present it to their peers
  - Other examples: case study, investigation of a specific law, regulation, or procedure

- **[Critiquing]**:
  - With a critique, students have to apply their knowledge and [problem-solving skills], especially if the critique must include possible solutions on which the group must agree
  - Students not only identify what's wrong, but also potential causes and how to fix them
  - Examples: welding instructor creates bad welds for students to examine; writing (grammar or prose); philosophy (logical conclusions); computer code (troubleshooting)
  - Peer evaluations or peer/group editing are also examples of critiquing

- **[Games and More]**:
  - Include [multiplayer games] which require players to collaborate. These make great test reviews
  - Examples: charades, Taboo, Headbandz/Heads-Up! where players are part of a team
  - Games typically based on individual performance (e.g., Jeopardy) can be modified to be more collaborative by using groups instead of individuals
  - [Group testing], where two or more students work on a test together, has shown promising results

2. Individual Work Versus Group Work.desc:
- The age-old question, 'Individual work or team work; which is better?' There are no definite answers, as different people have [different working styles]. Knowing when to go alone, and when to work together is vital.

- **[Individual Work]**:
  - It is better for a person to work alone for a task that requires [high concentration and focus]. Group work can cause unnecessary interruptions
  - The Coding War Games found that programmers tend to work [faster when working by themselves]. Designers, artists, writers, and engineers do their best work alone
  - Being in the [ZONE, also known as FLOW], where a person is fully immersed into an activity with focus, full involvement, and enjoyment, can only be achieved when working individually
  - When learning a new skill, it is better to do so alone. According to psychologist Anders Ericsson, the best way to master a skill is to go directly to the part that is [most challenging to you personally]
  - By working alone, you can [decide what to do and when to do it]

- **[Group Work]**:
  - Teamwork is vital for a [brainstorming session] because different people have different experiences and backgrounds. More ideas will be generated and productivity will increase due to motivation
  - We can see things from [different angles and directions] by listening to others. Collect all ideas, then choose the best one or connect ideas together
  - Teamwork can also [increase communication] between team members. Communication is very important for a team to work well
  - An open environment can be a great [support for team members during challenging times], as they can look to one another for support and guidance

- **[Individual Learning]** refers to the attainment of knowledge through [teacher instruction and individual practice] in a classroom.

5. Principles for Implementing Group Pro.desc:
- **[Preparing for Group Work]**:
  - Think carefully about how students will be [physically arranged in groups]. Will it be easy for groups to form? Will all students be comfortable? How will layout impact volume?
  - [Insist on professional, civil conduct] between students to respect differences and create an inclusive environment
  - Talk to students about their [past experiences with group work] and allow them to establish ground rules for successful collaboration. This can be done anonymously through note cards

- **[Designing the Group Activity]**:
  - [Identify the instructional objectives]. Determine what you want to achieve academically (knowledge of a topic) and socially (listening skills). The activity should relate closely to course objectives
  - For group learning to be effective, students need a clear sense that group work is serving the stated learning goals
  - Ask: What is the objective? How will it be furthered by group work? Is the activity challenging enough to require group work? Will the project require true collaboration?
  - [Make the task challenging]. Consider giving a relatively easy task early to arouse interest, then more challenging tasks. By pooling resources and dealing with differences, groups develop more sophisticated products
  - [Assign group tasks that encourage involvement, interdependence, and fair division of labour]. All members should feel personal responsibility for team success. This is called [positive interdependence]
  - [Allocate essential resources across the group] so members are required to share information (e.g., jigsaw method). Randomly select one person to speak for the group, or assign different roles (recorder, spokesperson, summarizer, checker, timekeeper, etc.)
  - [Specify common rewards] for the group, such as a group mark
  - [Decide on group size]. Groups of four to five tend to balance diversity, productivity, participation, and cohesion. The less skillful the members, the smaller the groups should be
  - [Decide how you will divide students into groups]. Division based on proximity or student choice is quickest but often means students work with friends. To increase diversity, randomly assign students by counting off. For some tasks, you might want to assign groups yourself based on data cards

4. Examples of Group Activities in the C.desc:
- **[Stump Your Partner]**:
  - Students take a minute to create a [challenging question] based on lecture content
  - Students pose the question to the person sitting next to them
  - To take further, ask students to write down questions and hand them in. These can be used to create tests or exams, or reviewed to gauge student understanding

- **[Think-Pair-Share/Write-Pair-Share]**:
  - The instructor poses a question that demands [analysis, evaluation, or synthesis]
  - Students take a few minutes to think through an appropriate response
  - Students turn to a partner (or small groups) and share their responses
  - Take further by asking students to find someone with a different answer and convince them to change their mind
  - Responses are shared with the entire class during follow-up discussion

- **[Catch-Up]**:
  - Stop at a transition point in your lecture
  - Have students turn to a partner or work in small groups to [compare notes and ask clarifying questions]
  - After a few minutes, open the floor to a few questions

- **[Fishbowl Debate]**:
  - Ask students to sit in groups of three
  - [Assign roles]: person on left takes one position, person on right takes opposite position, person in middle takes notes and decides which side is most convincing and provides an argument
  - Debrief by calling on a few groups to summarize discussions

- **[Case Study]**:
  - Create [four to five case studies] of similar difficulty
  - Have students work in groups of four or five to work through and analyze their case study
  - Provide 10-15 minutes (or adequate time) to work through cases
  - Walk around and address any questions
  - Call on groups randomly and ask students to share their analysis. Continue until each case study has been addressed

- **[Team-Based Learning]**:
  - Start a course unit by giving students some tasks to complete, such as [reading or lab assignments]. Consider assigning these to be completed before class
  - Check students' comprehension with a quick multiple-choice quiz. Have students submit answers
  - Assign students to groups and have them review answers to reach consensus. Have each group submit one answered quiz
  - Record both individual and group assessment scores
  - Deliver a lecture targeting any misconceptions or gaps revealed
  - Give groups a challenging assignment, such as solving a problem or applying a theory to a real-world situation

8. Pros and Cons of Individual Projects.desc:
- **[Pros of Individual Projects]**:
  - Students' success is [entirely based on themselves], rather than on peers who may or may not contribute
  - Students have [more freedom to do a project on any topic] (within confines of assignment) because they don't have to select as a group
  - There are [no concerns about conflicts] among students since everyone works independently
  - Every student is responsible for his or her own learning, so students who may skate by during group projects will [actually do individual projects themselves] and learn a great deal
  - Teachers have [less to oversee] during individual projects because students work independently and will likely have fewer questions
  - Student can work at [his or her own pace]
  - They encourage [independent thinking and independent problem-solving skills]

- **[Cons of Individual Projects]**:
  - There is [no accountability amongst peers] for completing the project
  - [Less collaboration, communication, sharing of ideas], etc.
  - Projects must be [smaller and more manageable] for individuals
  - Presenting individual projects takes [far longer] than presenting group projects since there is one for every student

7. Pros and Cons of Group Projects.desc:
- **[Pros of Group Projects]**:
  - They encourage [teamwork and collaboration], incredibly important skills students will need long after graduation
  - They can help students learn to [break down certain tasks and allocate responsibilities]
  - They allow for group discussion, which often leads to [innovative ideas, challenging assumptions, and improved communication skills]
  - They are able to take on [more complex problems] than they could handle individually
  - They will utilize [problem-solving skills and conflict resolution] as they work together
  - More accountability happens—lower achieving students are usually [called out on their lack of contribution] because everyone's grade is at stake
  - Individuals' weaknesses don't affect the group because someone else often has a [strength that makes up for one person's weakness]

- **[Cons of Group Projects]**:
  - Some [quieter students may struggle] to contribute to the group
  - Groups may [allocate work unevenly] based on students' grades or knowledge, leaving some to do far more work than others
  - Poor communication skills or [scheduling conflicts can lead to poor performance]
  - Groups can often spend too much time [discussing ideas, organizing responsibilities, or just chatting] without getting much accomplished
  - Students are often required to ["grade" one another's contributions], which can lead to problems

6. Principles for Implementing Group Pro.desc:
- **[Introducing the Group Activity]**:
  - [Share your rationale] for using group work. Don't assume students know the pedagogical purpose. Explicitly connect activities to larger class themes and learning outcomes
  - [Have students form groups before giving instructions]. If you give instructions first, students may be too preoccupied with group membership to listen
  - [Facilitate some form of group cohesion]. Have students introduce themselves. For longer periods, consider an icebreaker to build teamwork
  - [Explain the task clearly]. Tell students exactly what they have to do and describe what the final product will look like. Prepare written or visual instructions including time estimations
  - [Set ground rules] for group interaction: respect, active listening, methods for decision-making. Consider making a group contract
  - [Let students ask questions]. Give them time to ask before they get to work

- **[Monitoring the Group Task]**:
  - [Monitor the groups but do not hover]. Circulate and answer questions. Listen for trends emerging to refer to during plenary discussion. Avoid interfering too soon; allow time for students to solve their own problems
  - [Expect a lot from your students]. Assume they do know and can do a great deal. Express confidence in them as you circulate
  - [Be slow to share what you know]. If a group is experiencing uncertainty, avoid giving answers. Let students struggle within reason to accomplish the task
  - [Clarify your role as facilitator]. If criticized for not contributing enough, consider if you have communicated your facilitator role clearly

- **[Ending the Group Task]**:
  - [Provide closure]. Students want to see how their work was useful. End with a plenary session for group reporting. Effective group reporting makes the difference between students feeling they are just going through paces versus engaged in powerful exchange
  - **[Oral reports]**: Have each group give one idea and rotate until no new ideas arise. Or have each group give most surprising insights or challenging questions. Record ideas to validate value
  - **[Written reports]**: Have groups record ideas on blackboard or flipchart paper posted on walls. Students circulate and read answers. Another variation: students rotate and add comments
  - [Model how you want students to participate]. When responding, model respect and sensitivity. Acknowledge and value opinions different from your own. Be willing to share own stories
  - [Connect ideas raised to course content and objectives]. Groups might not come up with intended ideas, so be flexible. Look for connection between group conclusions and course topic. Clarify and correct misconceptions
  - [Don't provide too much closure]. Leave some questions unanswered for further research or next class. This reflects the nature of knowledge
  - [Ask students to reflect on the group work process]. Either orally or in writing. This helps them discover what they learned and how they functioned in the group. It also gives you a sense of their response

1. Definition of Intervention Strategies.desc:
- The role of education for all children is to provide the experiences needed to learn skills that will lead to ["personal independence and social responsibility"] (National Research Council [NRC], 2001, p. 40).

- **[Educational intervention]** means providing students with the [support needed to acquire the skills] being taught by the educational system.

- It should address [functional skills, academic, cognitive, behavioural, and social skills] that directly affect the child's ability to access education.

- For students with autism spectrum disorders, the interventions often address some of the [core deficits] in the areas of communication, social skills, and behavioural differences.

- The interventions should be aimed at skills that need to be [acquired or that need to be performed more often] to lead to successful results.

3. Silent Signals as an Intervention Str.desc:
- Create [silent signals] to remind your students to pay attention and remain on task.

- These signals can be for your [whole class] or you can establish special signals for a particular student who needs extra behavioral support.

- Silent signals are an effective intervention because they quickly [reinforce behavioral expectations with minimal disruption].

- You can come up with signals for your class or your class can help you create signals that work best for your community.

- You can create signals to [express your expectations] for your students; and you can also create signals to allow your students to [express their needs to you].

- Example: signals for asking for the bathroom and getting water, or to let students know when you are with a student and should not be interrupted.

- Remember to keep silent signals [simple and quick].

5. Quiet Corrections as an Intervention.desc:
- When a student is off task, they are often [seeking attention], so it is important for teachers to remove the stage when addressing them.

- Avoid using [shame and intimidation] to correct a student.

- Instead, [quietly and quickly bend down and whisper] to the student what you would like them to do and the consequence they will receive if the expectation is not met, then move away.

- If the student still does not comply, [administer an appropriate consequence].

- Quiet corrections allow you to [remain in control of the situation] and keeps the public stage out of the student interaction.

11. Summary of Session Six.desc:
- **[Educational intervention]** means providing students with the support needed to acquire the skills being taught by the educational system and should address functional skills, academic, cognitive, behavioural, and social skills that directly affect the child's ability to access education.

- Some examples of educational interventions are:
  - [Routines]
  - [Silent signals]
  - [Proximity]
  - [Quiet corrections]
  - [Give students a task]
  - [Take a break]
  - [Positive phrasing]
  - [Tangible reinforcers]
  - [Statement of the behaviour you want to see]

6. Give Students a Task as an Interventi.desc:
- Negative student behavior affects your classroom and the other students.

- If you notice a student has a behavioral challenge at a certain time of day, consider [giving them a task or errand to complete] for you, for example sending a message to another teacher.

- This will give the student a [chance to reset and come back] and join the class.

- Also consider ways to encourage [leadership and peer interaction] by pairing the student up with a classmate as a helper on an academic task to encourage positive interactions and build community.

4. Proximity as an Intervention Strategy.desc:
- Proximity is another great [silent intervention].

- By simply [getting physically closer to a student], you can get them on task without having to give verbal instructions.

- Make it a habit to [circulate around your classroom] while students are completing tasks to keep them focused.

- You can even [rest your hand on their shoulder] to get the student's attention.

- Use proximity when teaching a lesson, during independent work, or in transitions to a new task.

9. State the Behavior You Want to See.desc:
- [Positively narrate the behavior you want to see] or acknowledge students who meet expectations right away.

- This [rewards positive behavior] and repeats the expectation for students who may not have heard the first time.

- For example, when students were lining up and not ready, quickly state the specific behavior 1-2 students were doing correctly. In no time other students where mimicking that behavior.

ex: focus on positive results of behavior

8. Positive Phrasing as an Intervention.desc:
- This refers to [focusing on the positive results] of a behavior rather than the negative.

- As teachers we can easily get into the habit of threatening students with statements that say, "If you don't...then I will..." This type of phrase is [negatively stated and creates tension].

- Instead, [describe the positive impact] of a behavior.

- This also works when establishing class rules. Class rules should be [positively phrased], instead of "Don't run in the halls" the rule can be, "We walk in the halls."

7. Take a Break as an Intervention Strat.desc:
- Students benefit from a [3-5 minute break] to reset and get focused.

- Consider allowing students [rest and reset] during an activity or before transitioning to a new task.

- [Brain breaks] are helpful and fun.

2. Routines as an Intervention Strategy.desc:
- Set [clear routines] for everything you would like students to do in your classroom.

- Although it can be tedious, be [explicit about everything]. Do not assume that students know the expectations for your classroom.

- Be sure to [show them how you would like things to be done].

- Give students [multiple opportunities to practice] classroom routines; provide ongoing support for routines and behaviors.

- [Reinforce expected behaviors] and explain the consequences if the expectations are not met.

- Teach your routines and expectations in a way that allows you to [differentiate ignorance versus defiance]. Students often get in trouble because they genuinely did not know what they were expected to do.

10. Tangible Reinforcers as an Intervent.desc:
- [Rewards] are an effective way to encourage positive behavior.

- Rewards can be [edible treats, toys, or a desired activity].

- Be sure that you set [clear guidelines] for how to earn rewards.

- Set [realistic goals] so students can earn the reward consistently, to maintain motivation.

- Get [student input] so the rewards are items or activities that the student desires.
